topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon had been a relentless grind. Every email, every phone call, every misplaced item felt like a deliberate slight. I stomped to the mailbox, a low growl rumbling in my chest, ready to confront whatever garbage awaited me. Instead, tucked beneath a crumpled flyer, was a card. Unsigned. My jaw tightened as I pulled it out. The bright, cheerful script felt like an insult against the backdrop of my mounting frustration. Who in the world thought they could just drop something like this in my mail? I crumpled the card in my fist, the paper protesting under the pressure. I stared at the blank space where a name should have been, my knuckles white. This was just… pointless.

[story 2]

I slammed the front door shut, the sound echoing the sudden spike of heat in my neck. I hadn't wanted to go out, but the sheer weight of the day had pushed me out into the biting evening air. When I reached the mailbox, it was a necessary evil, a chore I despised. And there it was: a card. White, slightly creased, bearing a perfectly rendered, saccharine message. My breath hitched. I snatched it up, my fingers brushing against the unfamiliar texture. I scanned the front, my eyes narrowing, searching desperately for a signature, a clue, anything to justify this intrusion. Finding none, I let out a sharp, humorless exhale. I dropped it back into the slot as if it were contaminated, turning sharply away before I could let the strange object disrupt the fragile composure I was trying to maintain.

[story 3]

My patience had evaporated somewhere between lunch and this moment. I approached the mailbox with a deliberate, stiff gait, every muscle in my body coiled tight. When I retrieved the mail, the sight of the card stopped me dead. It was unsolicited. Unmarked. A stark piece of paper in a system designed for utility, now bearing an unexpected personal touch. A tight knot formed in my stomach. I unfolded it slowly, my gaze sweeping over the simple design. There was no context, no explanation, just an empty space where a name should have been. A low, frustrated sound escaped my throat—a sound more like a hiss than a word. I crushed the card between my thumb and forefinger, the flimsy material yielding instantly under the force of my displeasure. I shoved it into the trash bin with unnecessary violence.
